questions.question: What distinguishes Seasonal Affective Disorder (SAD) from other forms of depression?
options: SAD is not real depression — just feeling a bit sad in winter
SAD is major depression with recurrent seasonal pattern (typically fall/winter onset, spring/summer remission)
SAD only affects people who live in polar regions
SAD can be cured by going outside for 5 minutes daily
questions.explanation: SAD is a subtype of major depression with recurrent seasonal pattern — typically fall/winter onset and spring/summer remission. Prevalence: 1–10% depending on latitude. Mechanism: reduced daylight disrupts circadian rhythms, suppresses serotonin, increases daytime melatonin. It's a clinical condition requiring professional diagnosis and treatment (light therapy, CBT-SAD, medication). Outdoor light exposure is adjunctive, not standalone treatment.

questions.question: Why is outdoor light exposure more effective for circadian regulation than indoor lighting, even on cloudy days?
options: There's no difference — indoor and outdoor light are equivalent
Even cloudy outdoor light (1,000–10,000 lux) is significantly brighter than indoor lighting (100–500 lux)
Only sunny outdoor light works — cloudy days provide no benefit
Outdoor light only helps if you stare directly at the sun
questions.explanation: Even overcast outdoor light provides 1,000–10,000 lux, which is 10–100× brighter than typical indoor lighting (100–500 lux). This intensity is sufficient to suppress daytime melatonin, boost cortisol awakening response, and anchor circadian rhythms for better sleep. Morning outdoor light exposure (first 1–2 hours after waking) for 15–30 minutes shows moderate evidence for mood regulation and SAD support.

questions.question: Which season poses the highest mental health risks in temperate climates according to the lesson?
options: Spring — because change is stressful
Summer — because heat is always dangerous
Winter — light deprivation, SAD onset, social isolation, vitamin D deficiency, circadian disruption
Autumn — because leaves falling symbolizes death
questions.explanation: Winter poses highest mental health risks in temperate/polar climates: (1) SAD onset (Oct–Feb), (2) reduced daylight (8–10 hours or less), (3) social isolation (cold reduces outdoor gatherings), (4) vitamin D deficiency (insufficient UVB at high latitudes), (5) circadian disruption, (6) reduced physical activity. Protective strategies: morning outdoor light, winter sports, layered gear, group activities for accountability.

questions.question: What is an evidence-supported winter outdoor engagement strategy for maintaining mental health in cold climates?
options: Avoid all outdoor activity until spring — indoor exercise is equivalent
Prioritize morning outdoor time (even 15 min) for circadian light exposure; add winter-specific activities (snowshoeing, winter hiking); use group accountability
Force yourself to spend 2+ hours outdoors daily regardless of temperature
Winter outdoor activity is only for professional athletes
questions.explanation: Evidence-supported winter strategies include: (1) morning outdoor light exposure (even 15 min walk) for circadian regulation and mood support, (2) winter-specific activities (snowshoeing, cross-country skiing, winter forest bathing), (3) appropriate gear (layered clothing, waterproof boots), (4) group accountability (walking groups, winter hiking challenges overcome solo motivation collapse), (5) shorter outings with safety protocols. Indoor exercise doesn't provide light exposure or circadian benefits.

questions.question: How is climate change affecting seasonal outdoor engagement patterns and mental health?
options: Climate change has no effect on outdoor mental health practices
Shifting seasonal baselines, extended wildfire smoke seasons, extreme heat days increasing — creating access barriers, unpredictability stress, and ecological anxiety
Climate change only affects outdoor sports, not mental health
Climate change makes outdoor activity easier year-round
questions.explanation: Climate change impacts: (1) shifting seasonal baselines (springs arriving weeks early, unpredictable weather), (2) wildfire smoke rendering outdoor activity unsafe for weeks-to-months in affected regions, (3) extreme heat days increasing (>90°F days up 130+ annually in some areas), (4) unpredictability creating planning stress, (5) ecological anxiety/grief from witnessing disruption. Adaptations: flexibility over rigid seasonal planning, air quality monitoring (AQI apps), indoor-outdoor hybrid spaces, advocacy alongside coping.
